catalog_card.title: Jeju West & South Full-Day Authentic Tour
catalog_card.shortCardDescription: A small-group day tour pairing Jeju's western and southern coasts in a single loop — Hallasan's 1100 Altitude Wetland (UNESCO), the hexagonal Jusangjeolli Cliff, Songaksan's panoramic viewpoint, Cheonjeyeon Waterfall, O'sulloc Tea Museum, and the Hyeopjae Beach café strip on the west coast. Same operator as the Southern UNESCO tour but with a more lifestyle-oriented framing — 'volcanic landscapes meet dramatic coastlines and trendy seaside cafés.'
catalog_card.subtitle: Hallasan UNESCO highland, Jusangjeolli Cliff, Songaksan rim, Cheonjeyeon Falls, O'sulloc tea, and Hyeopjae Beach — Jeju's west and south in one balanced day.

itineraryStops.name: Pickup (4 points across Jeju City)
itineraryStops.description: **Pickup is consolidated at four central Jeju City points** to minimize morning crisscrossing: **Jeju International Airport (제주공항)** for guests with same-day arrivals; **Jeju City Hall (제주시청)** for the Yeon-dong / Nohyeong / Old Town hotel cluster; **Hagwi-dong (하귀동)** as the westernmost pickup before the southbound stretch; and **Dongmun Traditional Market (동문시장)** as the optional eastern Jeju City pickup. Sequence is Hagwi → Airport → City Hall → Dongmun → southbound Route 1100 toward the Hallasan 1100 Altitude Wetland trailhead. The minicoach is **small-group (max ~14 pax)** with English-speaking guide. **Bilingual itinerary card** distributed at pickup, weather + Route 1100 closure brief at first stop. **Route 1100 closure check** is performed before departure — if closed for snow/ice, the 1100 Wetland stop substitutes to a lower-elevation alternate (typically Mysterious Road or Andeok Valley).
highlights: **4 pickup points** — Airport, City Hall, Hagwi, Dongmun
Sequence west→east minimizes morning coach distance
**Small-group minicoach (max 14 pax)** with English guide
Bilingual itinerary card + Route 1100 closure brief at first stop
**Winter contingency**: 1100 Wetland → Mysterious Road / Andeok Valley alternate
Total morning transit Jeju City → 1100 Wetland: ~50 min via Route 1100
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: Four pickup points cover Jeju City's hotel zones (airport, mid-city, old town, market district) without forcing guests across the city. Sequencing west→east lands the coach at 1100 Wetland ~10:20 — early enough to catch the morning alpine clarity before midday haze. **Route 1100 closure protocol** ensures guests are informed about winter contingency before departure — Jeju's only highest-altitude national highway is closed ~5–10 days/year for ice.

itineraryStops.name: Hallasan 1100 Altitude Wetland (Ramsar + UNESCO)
itineraryStops.category: Alpine Wetland — Ramsar Site + within Hallasan UNESCO World Heritage
itineraryStops.description: **Hallasan 1100 Altitude Wetland (한라산 1100고지 습지)** is a high-elevation alpine wetland system on the Hallasan plateau, located **at exactly 1,100 m above sea level** along **Route 1100 — the highest national highway in Korea** linking Jeju City to Seogwipo Jungmun. The site is a **Ramsar Wetland of International Importance (Site No. 1893, designated 12 October 2009)**, falling within the **Hallasan UNESCO World Heritage Site (2007)**, **Jeju Island UNESCO Biosphere Reserve (2002)**, and **Hallasan National Park (1970)** — making this one of the few sites globally with **four overlapping international/national designations**. The wetland comprises **16+ discontinuous freshwater marshes and pools** spread across a low-gradient plateau section that captures and retains rainwater, forming **small permanent lakes and peat bogs** that support diverse floristic assemblages with significant Jeju endemism. Recorded biodiversity includes **3 threatened bird species and 1 threatened plant species within Korea**, plus mammal, reptile, and insect species endemic to Jeju Island. The site received **900,000 visitors in 2008** (Mt Halla Visitor Centre data). Visitor experience: a **1-km wooden boardwalk loop** with observation decks, interpretive signage, and a small natural-history exhibit at the trailhead. **Autumn foliage (October–November)** and **winter snow scenes (December–February)** are the most photographed seasons; **spring + summer** offer wetland flora and endemic species visibility. **FREE admission**; adjacent **1100 Rest Area (1100 휴게소)** provides indoor restrooms + cafe + small souvenir shop. **Cultural and religious importance** to local communities is documented in the Ramsar listing — historical shaman pilgrimages tied to mountain-water spirits.

itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: **Alpine UNESCO opening anchor of the west-south authentic route**. Where the south-UNESCO sister tour (Tour 1) opens at Hallasan's **lower-elevation Eoseungsaengak trail** (1,169 m), this authentic-emphasis route opens at the **higher 1,100 m wetland** for a **fundamentally different Hallasan experience**: not a cone-climb but an **alpine wetland boardwalk** showcasing Jeju endemism + Ramsar-grade biodiversity. Route 1100 itself is the experience — Korea's highest national highway, with a winding ascent through Hallasan's western flank delivering panoramic foliage + snow scenes depending on season. Compared with **Yeongsil Trail (popular but 4.5-hour round-trip summit route)** or **Eorimok Trail (longer mid-elevation)**, the 1100 Wetland is **45-minute viable** and delivers UNESCO + Ramsar + Biosphere depth in a single boardwalk loop — the highest yield-per-minute alpine stop in Hallasan National Park.

itineraryStops.name: Jusangjeolli Cliff (UNESCO Geopark)
itineraryStops.description: **Daepo Jusangjeolli (대포 주상절리, 'Jisatgae')** is a **1 km stretch of hexagonal columnar-jointed basalt cliff** on the Jungmun coast — designated **Natural Monument No. 443 on 2005-01-06**. The columns rise **30–40 m** from the sea, packed in tightly fitted **5- to 7-sided polygons** (mostly hexagons) — a geometry produced when **molten basaltic lava from a Hallasan flank eruption (~250,000–140,000 years ago) reached the ocean and contracted as it cooled**, fracturing perpendicular to the cooling surface. Globally, columnar jointing is best known from **Giant's Causeway (UK)**, **Devils Postpile (US)**, and **Fingal's Cave (Scotland)** — Jusangjeolli is **East Asia's premier example** and the most accessible. The 800 m **boardwalk loop** delivers visitors to a railed cliff edge directly above the columns; **on high-tide days waves break against the columns and throw spray up to 20 m**, occasionally over the rail. Admission: **Adult ₩2,000 / Youth ₩1,000 / FREE under 7 and over 65**. The site is part of the **Jeju Global Geopark (UNESCO designated 2010-10)** — one of **13 official geosites** on the island, alongside Seongsan Ilchulbong, Manjanggul Lava Tube, and Suwolbong. The clifftop deck is **barrier-free** (wheelchair/stroller accessible). Three viewing platforms along the loop give different column angles: the **first platform** shows the columns in profile (best for the hexagonal-geometry shot), the **mid-platform** looks straight down for top-of-column patterns, and the **far platform** captures the 1 km coastal sweep with Hallasan as backdrop on clear days. **Photographic peak window: 14:00–16:00**, when westering sunlight lights the column faces frontally; sunset visits bring silhouette compositions but lose column-detail texture. Hours: **09:00–18:00 (last entry 17:30)**, year-round operation.

itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: **Mid-morning UNESCO Geopark anchor** of the west-south authentic route. Where the south-UNESCO sister tour places Jusangjeolli as a **late-afternoon golden-hour climax** (15:40 arrival), this authentic-emphasis route uses Jusangjeolli as the **mid-morning UNESCO Geopark transition** between the alpine 1100 Wetland and the southwest double-crater Songaksan — explicitly framing it as **the lava-flow downstream of the Hallasan we just visited**, completing a single geological narrative arc from alpine source to coastal solidification. The 11:35 arrival catches **post-morning-haze clarity** with strong vertical light on the basalt columns. Compared with the south-UNESCO tour's afternoon golden-hour framing, this earlier window suits photographers preferring **dramatic top-light on the column tops** rather than side-lit warm tones.

itineraryStops.name: Lunch — Daejeong / Andeok Local
itineraryStops.category: Midday Reset — South-West Jeju Local Cuisine
itineraryStops.description: **60-minute reset between Jusangjeolli and Songaksan** — Daejeong / Andeok corridor sits 10–15 min from both stops. Default rotation: **galchi-jorim (갈치조림, braised cutlassfish)** — Jeju's south coast is Korea's primary cutlassfish landing, with Daejeong harbor delivering daily catches; **mulhoe (물회)** in summer; **heuk-doeji gui (흑돼지구이, Jeju black pig BBQ)** year-round (Cultural Heritage Material No. 5, 2015); or — **west-south specialty** — **gosari yukgaejang (고사리 육개장, fern + beef spicy soup)** rooted in Jeju's mountain-fern foraging tradition, especially common in Daejeong-eup. **Vegetarian + halal-friendly options** available at one venue in the rotation — disclose at pickup. Lunch venue is **10-min coach to Songaksan** trailhead.

itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: Daejeong-eup positioning = 10-min hop to Songaksan. **Galchi from Daejeong harbor + gosari yukgaejang** are both regional specialties anchored in the Daejeong / Andeok tradition (Daejeong was the historical Jeong-eui-hyeon administrative seat for west-south Jeju, similar to Seongeup's role in east Jeju). The fern-foraging tradition ties to Jeju's **mountain-folk food heritage**, distinct from the coastal seafood emphasis of east Jeju. Pace also breaks the morning's alpine + Geopark walking before the afternoon's Songaksan loop + waterfall + tea museum + beach sunset.

itineraryStops.name: Songaksan Mountain (panoramic viewpoint)
itineraryStops.category: Double-Crater Volcanic Cone — 99 Peaks + Olle Trail 10 + WWII History
itineraryStops.description: **Songaksan Mountain (송악산)** is a **104 m double-crater parasitic volcanic cone** rising from Jeju's southwest tip in Daejeong-eup. The mountain is famous for its **'99 Peaks' (구십구봉)** folk count — a poetic naming of the dense scatter of small volcanic cones that radiate from the main mountain, formed by sequential eruption events that left **black + red volcanic ash deposits** still visible inside the crater rims. Geologically, Songaksan exhibits a **double-crater structure**: an **outer crater (~500 m diameter, ~1.7 km circumference)** and an **inner crater (~400 m diameter, ~69 m deep)** carved by a later subsidiary eruption inside the first. The visitor experience is the **2.8-km coastal loop trail** along the cliff edges, almost entirely **well-maintained wooden boardwalk + gentle stairs**, completing in **~75 minutes** at a moderate pace. The trail is the **central segment of Jeju Olle Trail Course 10 (Hwasun → Moseulpo)**. Panoramic views from the loop include — to the south — **Marado Island (Korea's southernmost point, 1.7 km offshore)** and **Gapado Island (flat barley-field island, 5.5 km offshore)**; to the southeast — **Hyeongje Islet ('Brother Islands') with their distinctive columnar-jointed twin rocks**; and to the northeast — **Sanbangsan Mountain (395 m tuff lava cone)** and **Hallasan (1,950 m, central island)**. **Historical layer**: the southern coastal cliffs hold **Imperial Japanese Army tunnels carved during WWII occupation** — kamikaze suicide-boat shelters and ammunition stores, similar to the 18 caves at Seongsan Ilchulbong. The tunnels are publicly accessible with interpretive signage. Wildlife: **free-roaming horses + ponies** graze the slopes (a Jeju visual signature dating to traditional pasture-land use). The **summit trail occasionally closes** for environmental conservation rotation; the coastal loop remains accessible year-round. **FREE admission**; small parking lot (often full at peak times). Strong sea winds even on sunny days — windbreaker recommended year-round.

itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: **Day's geological + historical climax** — and the catalog's only stop combining **double-crater volcanic cone geology + WWII colonial-history layer + 5-island panorama in a single 75-minute walk**. Songaksan sits **15 min west of Jusangjeolli** and **20 min east of Hyeopjae Beach** — the natural geographic pivot of the west-south route. Compared with **Sanbangsan Mountain (395 m, no summit trail) or Yongmeori Coast (tide-restricted access, 30-min walk)**, Songaksan is **104 m / 75-min loop / always-accessible coastal portion / WWII-layered**, making it the highest-yield southwest stop. The early-afternoon timing (13:40) catches **strong overhead light on the volcanic cliff strata** — distinct from typical sunset framing of the same stop, and saves Hyeopjae Beach for the actual sunset.

itineraryStops.name: Cheonjeyeon Waterfall (Pond of the Gods)
itineraryStops.description: **Cheonjeyeon Falls (천제연, '연 = pond + 천제 = Emperor of Heaven')** is a **three-tier waterfall** in Jungmun's volcanic gorge, designated **Natural Monument No. 378 (1993)** for its rare ecological combination. The name comes from a **Joseon-era Daoist legend that seven nymphs (선녀, *seonnyeo*) descended from heaven nightly to bathe in the upper pond** — a myth reinforced by the dramatic site's microclimate of perpetual mist. The **1st tier (22 m fall, 21 m deep pond)** drops into a circular basalt cauldron and only flows after heavy rain — most days the upper pond sits glassy and reflective. The **2nd tier (30 m fall) flows year-round** — the dominant photographic subject. The 3rd tier completes the descent to the sea. The **120 m / 78 m-high Seonimgyo Bridge (선임교, 'Bridge of the Seven Nymphs')** crosses the gorge midway through the loop — its cast-bronze relief panels along both sides depict **all seven nymphs in flight**, a literal architectural realization of the founding legend. Surrounding forest is a **rare-plant habitat** including **Korean toadlily (***Tricyrtis macropoda***)** and the endangered **Mankyua chejuense fern** (Jeju endemic, IUCN Critically Endangered). Loop length: **0.8 km, ~30 min walk** with several steep stair sections. Admission: **Adult ₩2,500 / Youth ₩1,350 / FREE under 7 + over 65**. Cheonjeyeon is also a member of the **'Yeongjusipgyeong (영주십경, 10 Scenic Wonders of Jeju)'** Joseon-era literati canon — alongside Hallasan summit, Seongsan Sunrise, and Jeongbang Falls — meaning today's southwest loop covers **2 of the 10 (Hallasan + Cheonjeyeon)** in a single day. The biennial **Jeju Chilseonyeo (Seven Nymphs) Festival** held at the bridge in May (every odd year) reenacts the descent legend with traditional dance and musical performance — reservations required when the festival coincides with a tour date. Hours **09:00–18:00** year-round (last entry 17:30).

itineraryStops.name: O'sulloc Tea Museum + Green Tea Fields
itineraryStops.category: Tea Heritage Museum — Korea's Largest Green Tea Field (Seogwang Dawon)
itineraryStops.description: **O'Sulloc Tea Museum (오설록 티 뮤지엄, '오설록' meaning 'meeting snow + green')** is the tea-culture flagship of **Amorepacific Corporation** — Korea's largest beauty company. Founder **Suh Sung-whan personally pioneered the project in 1979**, reclaiming volcanic ash farmland on Jeju's Seogwang plateau when conventional wisdom held tea couldn't be grown commercially in Korea. Today the **Seogwang Dawon plantation (서광다원)** is **Korea's largest green-tea field**, producing **~700 tonnes annually** across Amorepacific's three Jeju estates. The museum building **opened 2001** in the plantation's heart and was named to **designboom's Top 10 art museums** for its minimalist green-roofed pavilion-amid-tea-rows design. **FREE admission**, daily **09:00–18:00**, ~**2 million annual visitors**. The visit splits naturally into three segments: **(1) Museum interior** — tea production process, antique tea-ware collection from Tang dynasty (618–907 CE) onward, and rooftop observation deck with panoramic plantation view; **(2) Tea House cafe** — signature **green tea rolling cake (오설록 롤케이크)** and **green tea ice cream** served from the tea-leaf harvest year directly; **(3) Plantation walking paths** — gravel rows between tea bushes, popular for sunset photography at the western edge. **Innisfree Jeju House** (Amorepacific's natural cosmetics brand) is a **5-minute walk** for guests interested in the broader brand ecosystem — also free admission with optional tangerine soap DIY workshop (₩15,000). The museum building itself, designed by Korean architect **Mass Studies (Cho Min-suk)**, takes a deliberately low-profile horizontal form that mirrors the tea-row geometry rather than competing with the agricultural landscape — the architectural deference earning it both the designboom Top 10 listing and ongoing use as a case study in Korean contemporary museum design. Optional add-on: the **Osulloc Tea Class (₩30,000 per person, 60 min, advance booking required)** — guided tea-flight tasting with brewing technique demonstration on Korean ceramics, available daily **10:00–16:00**, English-speaking instruction on Wed/Sat sessions.

itineraryStops.name: Hyeopjae Beach + west-coast café strip
itineraryStops.category: West-Coast Sunset Beach — Twin-Beach + Biyangdo Island Panorama
itineraryStops.description: **Hyeopjae Beach (협재 해수욕장)** is one of **Jeju's Top 3 west-coast beaches** and broadly ranked among the island's overall **Top 10 beaches**, defined by its **distinctive silvery-white sand** — formed from **crushed seashells + coral fragments mixed with fine sand**, producing a luminescence rare on the Korean Peninsula. The beach forms a **9 km shoreline** when paired with its **twin-beach companion Geumneung Beach (immediately south, walkable along the same shore, less crowded)** — the two-beach pairing is one of the catalog's signature photographic windows. The signature view is **Biyangdo Island (비양도)** — a **1.2-km offshore volcanic island with ~200 permanent residents**, accessible by **20-minute ferry from Hallim Port**. Biyangdo is undergoing **Korea's first carbon-free island certification process**, an environmental policy showcase. The beach's **emerald-turquoise water** comes from the shallow coral-sand seabed, and at **low tide, tidal pools reveal hermit crabs, small shrimp, and juvenile fish** — Biyangdo can appear 'walkable' across the shallow expanse. **Pine forest backdrop (Songlim)** behind the beach offers free camping ground and shaded walking paths. Behind the dunes runs Jeju's most-developed **west-coast café strip** — concentrated cafe + restaurant + boutique architecture catering to domestic tourism. **Sunset behind Biyangdo over emerald water** is the visual signature and the catalog's premier west-coast sunset framing. **FREE admission**; **lifeguarded swim period July 1 – August 31**; ramp-style **wheelchair access** at multiple beach points; public restrooms + changing rooms + showers + equipment rentals year-round. Adjacent **Hallim Park** (botanical garden + lava caves + folk village, separate ₩10,000 admission, 1.5 km away) is occasionally substituted in winter.
highlights: **Top 3 west-coast Jeju beaches / Top 10 overall** — emerald-turquoise water + silvery sand
**Distinctive silvery-white sand** — crushed seashells + coral fragments

**Tidal pools at low tide** — hermit crabs, shrimp, juvenile fish; Biyangdo 'walkable' optical effect

timeUsed: Main beach walk + photo windows — ~20 min
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: **West-coast sunset finale of the authentic route**. Where the south-UNESCO sister tour ends inland at Osulloc tea fields, this authentic-emphasis route ends at the **Jeju west-coast sunset signature** — Biyangdo Island silhouetted over emerald-turquoise water, with the silvery-white sand catching late-afternoon golden tones. Geographic placement: Hyeopjae sits **30 min north of Osulloc** along Route 1132, looping the day naturally back toward Jeju City via the west coast. Compared with **Iho Tewoo Beach (Jeju City, urban + horse statues)** or **Geumneung Beach (twin neighbor, less amenity development)**, Hyeopjae is **on-route, sunset-anchored, Biyangdo-framed, and amenity-rich** — the highest-yield west-coast sunset stop. The 17:30 arrival aligns with **typical sunset windows**: ~17:30 (Dec) to ~19:50 (Jun); sunset photography window varies by season but always falls within the 60-min dwell.

routeShapeIntro.title: How this day flows
routeShapeIntro.subtitle: A west-and-south Jeju loop with a deliberately less heritage-heavy framing than the operator's Southern UNESCO tour. Where the Southern tour opens with the Eoseungsaengak Trail and emphasizes UNESCO labels, this 'Authentic' version opens at Hallasan's 1100 Wetland (still UNESCO, but more boardwalk than hike) and closes with the west-coast café strip rather than O'sulloc alone. The result is a route with the same heritage anchors (Hallasan, Jusangjeolli, Cheonjeyeon) but a softer pace and a stronger sense of contemporary Jeju lifestyle. Suited to visitors who want the iconic stops plus the chance to actually experience Jeju as Korean visitors do — coastal cafés, sunset viewpoints, scenic drives.

routeLogicSections.title: Why this route is built this way

items.label: UNESCO without the climb
items.detail: Most UNESCO Hallasan tours involve at least a 90-minute trail walk. This route uses the 1100 Altitude Wetland — same UNESCO biosphere, same volcanic landscape, but a flat boardwalk loop accessible to almost anyone. Visitors who want the heritage credit without the fitness commitment get it here.

items.label: Café-strip finale beats museum-only ending
items.detail: The standard southern circuits end at O'sulloc Tea Museum, which closes at 18:00 and feels like the day winding down. This route adds Hyeopjae Beach and its café strip as a closing stop — meaning the day ends on a sunset coastal café rather than walking back to a coach in a parking lot. A small detail with significant emotional payoff.

items.label: Two coasts in one day
items.detail: Most Jeju day tours stay on one coast. This route deliberately covers both — south coast (Jusangjeolli, Cheonjeyeon, Songaksan) and west coast (O'sulloc, Hyeopjae). For 1-day Jeju visitors, this is the route that maximizes coverage at the cost of slightly longer drive time between stops.

items.label: Same operator, same no-shopping policy
items.detail: This tour is run by the same operator as our Southern UNESCO tour — Love Korea Tours. The 'no shopping' clause and the 4-pickup-point system carry over. Reviews consistently mention guides Steven, Chloe, and Sunny by name across both products.

routeLogicSections.title: How the walking load actually feels

items.label: 1100 Wetland is the gentlest stop
items.detail: A flat boardwalk loop of about 1 km. Suitable for all ages and any fitness level. Wheelchair-friendly on the boardwalk.

items.label: Songaksan rim is the longest active walk
items.detail: A 2.6 km coastal rim circuit with low elevation gain. Allow 60-90 minutes for the full loop; a 1.5 km half-loop hits the strongest viewpoints if time is tight.

items.label: Cheonjeyeon stairs are the only demanding section
items.detail: The upper viewing platform requires no stairs. Reaching the second and third waterfall tiers needs about 80 stairs down and back up.

items.label: 1100 Road closes in heavy snow
items.detail: Typically 1-3 days per winter. Operator substitutes Sanbangsan Mountain temple complex or extra time at Hyeopjae if the road is closed.

items.label: Summer afternoons at Hyeopjae are busiest
items.detail: Café-strip seating fills between 16:00 and 18:00 in July and August. Expect queue waits at the most popular cafés if visiting in peak summer.

items.label: Cheonjeyeon water flow varies by season
items.detail: June through September is the strongest flow period after summer rain. Winter sees lower flow but clearer photographic conditions.

staticQuestions.question: How is this different from our Southern UNESCO tour?
staticQuestions.answer: Same operator. The Southern UNESCO tour is more heritage-focused (Eoseungsaengak Trail, Camellia Hill, Cheonjeyeon, Jusangjeolli, O'sulloc). This West & South Authentic tour swaps the trail for a boardwalk wetland and adds Songaksan + Hyeopjae café strip — softer pace, broader coastal coverage, more 'lived-in Jeju' feel. Either is a strong choice; choose Southern for heritage, this one for atmosphere.

staticQuestions.question: What if Hallasan's 1100 Road is closed?
staticQuestions.answer: Heavy snow can close the 1100 Road for safety (typically 1–3 days per winter). The operator swaps the wetland stop for an alternate — usually Sanbangsan Mountain temple complex or extra time at Hyeopjae. The route still runs.

staticQuestions.question: Can I just spend the whole afternoon at Hyeopjae?
staticQuestions.answer: No — the format is a fixed circuit with ≈45 minutes at Hyeopjae. If you want a flexible café-and-beach day, a private driver-guide is the better choice. This product is for visitors who want both the heritage stops and a taste of the café scene without choosing between them.

staticQuestions.question: Is lunch included?
staticQuestions.answer: No — lunch is at your own expense (~₩15,000). The guide will recommend a local Jeju restaurant and assist with vegetarian, vegan, halal, or allergy needs if you advise at booking.

staticQuestions.question: When are the Hyeopjae cafés open?
staticQuestions.answer: Most Hyeopjae café strip venues are open 10:00–22:00 year-round. The 17:30–18:15 stop window typically lands at sunset hour, which is the most photogenic time. Some cafés have terrace seating directly facing Biyangdo Island.

staticQuestions.question: Can I bring luggage on the airport pickup?
staticQuestions.answer: Yes. Notify the operator of luggage volume in advance. The mini-coach has dedicated luggage space; visitors arriving on early-morning flights often join directly with bags.

staticQuestions.question: Is this a good choice for first-time visitors to Jeju?
staticQuestions.answer: Yes — it covers the most distinctive stops in the area in a single balanced day.

staticQuestions.question: Is the walking difficult?
staticQuestions.answer: Light to moderate. The 1100 Wetland boardwalk is flat (≈ 1 km loop). Jusangjeolli is a railed boardwalk. Songaksan rim is 2.6 km but flat. Cheonjeyeon has 80+ stairs down to the second falls and back. Hyeopjae is flat sand. Closed-toe shoes recommended; not stroller-friendly at Cheonjeyeon.

staticQuestions.question: Is this tour suitable for families?
staticQuestions.answer: Yes — works well for families with children comfortable with a 9-hour day. Cheonjeyeon's stairs are the only physically demanding section. Kids enjoy Hyeopjae Beach. Lunch handles allergies and dietary needs (notify at booking).

staticQuestions.question: Can older adults join comfortably?
staticQuestions.answer: Yes for active seniors. The 1100 Wetland boardwalk is flat. Songaksan rim is 2.6 km flat. Cheonjeyeon's 80+ stairs are skippable from the upper platform. Less ideal for low-mobility travelers needing wheelchairs — most boardwalks are wheelchair-friendly but Cheonjeyeon is not.

staticQuestions.question: What's the recommended minimum age?
staticQuestions.answer: Best fit is age 6+. Kids should manage a 9-hour day with a 2.6 km Songaksan rim walk and Cheonjeyeon stairs. Younger children join with carriers. Hyeopjae Beach + O'sulloc green-tea ice cream are typical hits.

matching_metadata.audit_summary: West-south Jeju authentic loop covering 4 distinct international/national designation layers in one day: Ramsar Wetland of International Importance (1100 Altitude Wetland, Site No. 1893), within Hallasan UNESCO World Heritage 2007 + Biosphere 2002 + National Park 1970 (1100 Wetland triple-overlap), Natural Monument No. 443 (Daepo Jusangjeolli columnar-jointing), and Natural Monument No. 378 (Cheonjeyeon Falls). Plus Songaksan's 104 m double-crater volcanic cone with WWII colonial-history coastal tunnels and Hyeopjae Beach's twin-beach + Biyangdo Island west-coast sunset finale. Alpine + double-crater + west-coast counterpart to the south-UNESCO sister tour, which emphasizes Hallasan Eoseungsaengak + Camellia Hill botanical layer + heritage tea finale.

matching_metadata.differentiation_vs_south_unesco_sister: Same Jusangjeolli + Cheonjeyeon + Osulloc anchors, but **alpine Ramsar wetland + double-crater volcano + west-coast beach sunset emphasis** (1100 Wetland + Songaksan + Hyeopjae) vs. sister's **lower-elevation Hallasan trail + botanical + UNESCO arc emphasis** (Eoseungsaengak + Camellia Hill). This tour = 4 international/national designations + Songaksan double-crater + Hyeopjae sunset = catalog's most-varied terrain progression (alpine → coastal). Sister = 5 UNESCO/Natural Monument anchors + year-round bloom calendar + tea finale = catalog's densest UNESCO concentration. Pair complementarily for guests with 2 days on Jeju.

itineraryStops.name: O'sulloc Tea Museum + Green Tea Fields
itineraryStops.description: Jeju's signature green-tea estate, founded in 1979. The museum traces Korean tea culture; the surrounding tea fields are an iconic photo backdrop. Free admission. The on-site café serves green tea ice cream, tea-leaf cakes, and matcha lattes.

itineraryStops.name: Hyeopjae Beach + west-coast café strip
itineraryStops.description: The closing stop and the route's defining 'café vibes' moment. Hyeopjae Beach is one of Jeju's most photographed white-sand beaches, with views of Biyangdo Island offshore. The adjacent café strip — known locally as the Hyeopjae or Geumneung café district — is one of Jeju's most loved coastal café clusters. Free time for a sunset coffee or ice cream before the drive back to Jeju City.

routeShapeIntro.title: How this day flows
routeShapeIntro.subtitle: A west-and-south Jeju loop with a deliberately less heritage-heavy framing than the operator's Southern UNESCO tour. Where the Southern tour opens with the Eoseungsaengak Trail and emphasizes UNESCO labels, this 'Authentic' version opens at Hallasan's 1100 Wetland (still UNESCO, but more boardwalk than hike) and closes with the west-coast café strip rather than O'sulloc alone. The result is a route with the same heritage anchors (Hallasan, Jusangjeolli, Cheonjeyeon) but a softer pace and a stronger sense of contemporary Jeju lifestyle. Suited to visitors who want the iconic stops plus the chance to actually experience Jeju as Korean visitors do — coastal cafés, sunset viewpoints, scenic drives.

bestFor: Travelers wanting UNESCO Hallasan and the Jeju café-beach experience in one day
Photographers chasing variety (highland wetland → coastal cliffs → tea fields → white-sand beach)
First-time Jeju visitors with two or fewer island days
Couples and adult families wanting a balanced, well-paced full-day route
Travelers who prefer fixed-itinerary structure over private-driver flexibility
lessIdealFor: Visitors who want extended free time at one stop (the format is a fixed circuit)
Same-day cruise passengers — the 17:30–18:15 Hyeopjae finish conflicts with most cruise sailaway windows
Travelers wanting deep Hallasan trekking (this route is viewpoint-only at the 1100 Wetland)
Visitors needing wheelchair access at every stop (Cheonjeyeon has ≈ 80 stairs; Songaksan rim is 2.6 km of unpaved path)
Solo budget travelers (small-group per-person pricing; the relaxed pace fits pairs and families better)

routeLogicSections.title: Why this route is built this way

items.label: UNESCO without the climb
items.detail: Most UNESCO Hallasan tours involve at least a 90-minute trail walk. This route uses the 1100 Altitude Wetland — same UNESCO biosphere, same volcanic landscape, but a flat boardwalk loop accessible to almost anyone. Visitors who want the heritage credit without the fitness commitment get it here.

items.label: Café-strip finale beats museum-only ending
items.detail: The standard southern circuits end at O'sulloc Tea Museum, which closes at 18:00 and feels like the day winding down. This route adds Hyeopjae Beach and its café strip as a closing stop — meaning the day ends on a sunset coastal café rather than walking back to a coach in a parking lot. A small detail with significant emotional payoff.

items.label: Two coasts in one day
items.detail: Most Jeju day tours stay on one coast. This route deliberately covers both — south coast (Jusangjeolli, Cheonjeyeon, Songaksan) and west coast (O'sulloc, Hyeopjae). For 1-day Jeju visitors, this is the route that maximizes coverage at the cost of slightly longer drive time between stops.

items.label: Same operator, same no-shopping policy
items.detail: This tour is run by the same operator as our Southern UNESCO tour — Love Korea Tours. The 'no shopping' clause and the 4-pickup-point system carry over. Reviews consistently mention guides Steven, Chloe, and Sunny by name across both products.

routeLogicSections.title: How the walking load actually feels

items.label: 1100 Wetland is the gentlest stop
items.detail: A flat boardwalk loop of about 1 km. Suitable for all ages and any fitness level. Wheelchair-friendly on the boardwalk.

items.label: Songaksan rim is the longest active walk
items.detail: A 2.6 km coastal rim circuit with low elevation gain. Allow 60-90 minutes for the full loop; a 1.5 km half-loop hits the strongest viewpoints if time is tight.

items.label: Cheonjeyeon stairs are the only demanding section
items.detail: The upper viewing platform requires no stairs. Reaching the second and third waterfall tiers needs about 80 stairs down and back up.

items.label: 1100 Road closes in heavy snow
items.detail: Typically 1-3 days per winter. Operator substitutes Sanbangsan Mountain temple complex or extra time at Hyeopjae if the road is closed.

items.label: Summer afternoons at Hyeopjae are busiest
items.detail: Café-strip seating fills between 16:00 and 18:00 in July and August. Expect queue waits at the most popular cafés if visiting in peak summer.

items.label: Cheonjeyeon water flow varies by season
items.detail: June through September is the strongest flow period after summer rain. Winter sees lower flow but clearer photographic conditions.

practicalAccordionItems.title: What's included
practicalAccordionItems.content: Air-conditioned mini-coach, certified English/Chinese-speaking guide, attraction admissions (Cheonjeyeon, Jusangjeolli; O'sulloc and Hyeopjae are free entry), fuel/tolls/parking. Not included: lunch (~₩15,000), café purchases at Hyeopjae (optional), tips.

practicalAccordionItems.title: Pickup & drop-off
practicalAccordionItems.content: Sequential pickup at four points: Ocean Suites Jeju (08:30), Jeju Airport Gate 3 (08:45), LOTTE City (08:55), Shilla Duty-Free (09:05). Choose the closest at booking. Arrive 10 minutes early. Late arrivals over 10 minutes are non-refundable. Day-before WhatsApp confirmation.

practicalAccordionItems.title: Walking & terrain
practicalAccordionItems.content: Light to moderate. Hallasan 1100 Wetland is a flat boardwalk loop. Jusangjeolli is boardwalk with railings. Songaksan rim walk is 2.6 km but flat. Cheonjeyeon has stairs. Hyeopjae Beach is flat sand walking. Total walking ≈ 5 km (mostly flat). Comfortable closed-toe shoes essential; sandals OK at the beach finale.

practicalAccordionItems.title: Cancellation
practicalAccordionItems.content: Free cancellation up to 24 hours before pickup. Same-day cancellations and no-shows non-refundable. Hallasan 1100 Road may close in heavy snow — in that case the operator swaps the wetland for an alternate Hallasan trailhead or skips ahead to add more time at Hyeopjae.

practicalAccordionItems.title: Drop-off options
practicalAccordionItems.content: Five drop-off points: Shilla Duty-Free, LOTTE City Hotel, Jeju Airport, Ocean Suites, Dongmun Traditional Market. The west-coast finale means slightly later drop-off times (≈19:00) compared to other operator tours.

staticQuestions.question: How is this different from our Southern UNESCO tour?
staticQuestions.answer: Same operator. The Southern UNESCO tour is more heritage-focused (Eoseungsaengak Trail, Camellia Hill, Cheonjeyeon, Jusangjeolli, O'sulloc). This West & South Authentic tour swaps the trail for a boardwalk wetland and adds Songaksan + Hyeopjae café strip — softer pace, broader coastal coverage, more 'lived-in Jeju' feel. Either is a strong choice; choose Southern for heritage, this one for atmosphere.

staticQuestions.question: What if Hallasan's 1100 Road is closed?
staticQuestions.answer: Heavy snow can close the 1100 Road for safety (typically 1–3 days per winter). The operator swaps the wetland stop for an alternate — usually Sanbangsan Mountain temple complex or extra time at Hyeopjae. The route still runs.

staticQuestions.question: Can I just spend the whole afternoon at Hyeopjae?
staticQuestions.answer: No — the format is a fixed circuit with ≈45 minutes at Hyeopjae. If you want a flexible café-and-beach day, a private driver-guide is the better choice. This product is for visitors who want both the heritage stops and a taste of the café scene without choosing between them.

staticQuestions.question: Is lunch included?
staticQuestions.answer: No — lunch is at your own expense (~₩15,000). The guide will recommend a local Jeju restaurant and assist with vegetarian, vegan, halal, or allergy needs if you advise at booking.

staticQuestions.question: When are the Hyeopjae cafés open?
staticQuestions.answer: Most Hyeopjae café strip venues are open 10:00–22:00 year-round. The 17:30–18:15 stop window typically lands at sunset hour, which is the most photogenic time. Some cafés have terrace seating directly facing Biyangdo Island.

staticQuestions.question: Can I bring luggage on the airport pickup?
staticQuestions.answer: Yes. Notify the operator of luggage volume in advance. The mini-coach has dedicated luggage space; visitors arriving on early-morning flights often join directly with bags.

staticQuestions.question: Is this a good choice for first-time visitors to Jeju?
staticQuestions.answer: Yes — it covers the most distinctive stops in the area in a single balanced day.

staticQuestions.question: Is the walking difficult?
staticQuestions.answer: Light to moderate. The 1100 Wetland boardwalk is flat (≈ 1 km loop). Jusangjeolli is a railed boardwalk. Songaksan rim is 2.6 km but flat. Cheonjeyeon has 80+ stairs down to the second falls and back. Hyeopjae is flat sand. Closed-toe shoes recommended; not stroller-friendly at Cheonjeyeon.

staticQuestions.question: Is this tour suitable for families?
staticQuestions.answer: Yes — works well for families with children comfortable with a 9-hour day. Cheonjeyeon's stairs are the only physically demanding section. Kids enjoy Hyeopjae Beach. Lunch handles allergies and dietary needs (notify at booking).

staticQuestions.question: Can older adults join comfortably?
staticQuestions.answer: Yes for active seniors. The 1100 Wetland boardwalk is flat. Songaksan rim is 2.6 km flat. Cheonjeyeon's 80+ stairs are skippable from the upper platform. Less ideal for low-mobility travelers needing wheelchairs — most boardwalks are wheelchair-friendly but Cheonjeyeon is not.

staticQuestions.question: What's the recommended minimum age?
staticQuestions.answer: Best fit is age 6+. Kids should manage a 9-hour day with a 2.6 km Songaksan rim walk and Cheonjeyeon stairs. Younger children join with carriers. Hyeopjae Beach + O'sulloc green-tea ice cream are typical hits.

_score_audit._methodology: 0-1 float scale. Indoor ratio computed time-weighted per Jason's methodology.

unesco_fit.reasoning: Verified: 2 UNESCO-related stops — 1100 Wetland (inside Hallasan UNESCO + Ramsar) + Jusangjeolli (UNESCO Global Geopark). Songaksan, Cheonjeyeon, O'sulloc, Hyeopjae are non-UNESCO. 2/7 = 0.85 (strong UNESCO presence but not pure).

walking_level.reasoning: Verified totals: 1100 Wetland 1 km flat boardwalk, Jusangjeolli 0.5 km flat, Songaksan 2.8 km + 108 m elevation, Cheonjeyeon 0.5 km + stairs, O'sulloc 1 km flat, Hyeopjae 0.5 km flat. Total ~6 km + Songaksan elevation. 0.65 verified — Songaksan is the demanding stop.

scenic_level.reasoning: UPGRADED — verified 7 distinct landscapes: alpine wetland boardwalk, basalt columnar coast, coastal cliff trail with islands view, three-tier waterfall, tea fields, west-coast emerald beach. Most landscape-diverse Jeju route. 0.95.

photo_level.reasoning: Verified: 7 hero shots — wetland boardwalk + Hallasan, basalt columns, Songaksan with Sanbangsan + Marado, nymph bridge, tea fields, Biyangdo + emerald water. Among most photogenic Jeju routes.

iconic_landmark_fit.reasoning: Verified: 3 universally iconic (Jusangjeolli, O'sulloc, Hyeopjae); 1100 Wetland regionally known; Songaksan + Cheonjeyeon famous to Jeju visitors. 0.85 calibrated.

stroller_fit.reasoning: Verified: 1100 Wetland boardwalk (good), Jusangjeolli boardwalk (good), Songaksan boardwalk (mostly good — gentle stairs), O'sulloc flat (good), Hyeopjae beach ramp (good), Cheonjeyeon stairs (NOT stroller-friendly). 5/6 substantive stroller-friendly = 0.55.

mobility_friendly_fit.reasoning: Same logic — 5/6 mobility-friendly with Cheonjeyeon stairs being the exception. Slight upgrade beyond mechanical 0.6 not warranted because Songaksan elevation also limits some seniors.

indoor_ratio.reasoning: MAJOR CORRECTION using time-weighted method: 1100 Wetland 10%, Jusangjeolli 0%, Songaksan 0%, Cheonjeyeon 5%, O'sulloc 50%, Hyeopjae 0%. Without lunch: 30/330 ≈ 0.09. With lunch: 78/390 = 0.20. Catalog value 0.15 represents both — predominantly outdoor route. Mechanical Phase 1 conversion 0.4 was significantly overstated.

weather_sensitivity.reasoning: UPGRADED — verified ~85% outdoor stop time. 1100 Wetland subject to road closure in winter; Songaksan exposed sun + wind; Hyeopjae beach weather-dependent. Highest weather sensitivity in catalog after pure-beach tours.

rain_fit.reasoning: DOWNGRADED — only O'sulloc Tea Museum interior is reliable rain refuge. 1100 Wetland + Songaksan + Cheonjeyeon + Hyeopjae all rain-affected; Jusangjeolli also exposed. Verified poor rain hedge.

intangible_heritage_fit.reasoning: DOWNGRADED — only Cheonjeyeon Falls has clear intangible heritage (Chilseonyeo Festival). Songaksan has WWII forced-labor memorial significance but not UNESCO ICH category. Most of the route is natural heritage. 0.45 verified.

beach_fit.reasoning: UPGRADED — Hyeopjae IS the dedicated beach finale (one of Jeju's iconic west-coast beaches). 0.85 reflects beach as a major route theme.

first_time_fit.reasoning: Verified: covers UNESCO mountain wetland + columnar geopark + coastal trail + waterfall + tea + beach = 6 distinct Jeju essentials in one day. 0.95 — excellent first-Jeju overview, particularly comprehensive for natural-heritage focus.

value_for_money_fit.reasoning: Verified fees: 1100 Wetland free + Jusangjeolli ₩2,000 + Songaksan free + Cheonjeyeon ₩2,500 + O'sulloc free + Hyeopjae free = ~₩4,500 total per adult. Lowest fee load in catalog (vs Camellia Hill ₩12,000 in southern-top tour). 0.95 — excellent value.

young_kids_fit.reasoning: DOWNGRADED — Songaksan 2.8 km + 108 m climb taxing for under-7s; Cheonjeyeon stairs same issue. 1100 Wetland + Jusangjeolli + O'sulloc + Hyeopjae kid-friendly. 4/6 kid-friendly = 0.50.

senior_fit.reasoning: Slight downgrade — Songaksan + Cheonjeyeon limit some seniors. Other stops fully accessible. 0.55 verified.

culture_level.reasoning: Verified: O'sulloc tea + Cheonjeyeon legend + Songaksan WWII memorial. Most of route is natural heritage. 0.55 calibrated.

nature_fit.reasoning: VERIFIED 1.0 — every stop is nature-anchored: Ramsar wetland, basalt cliff, coastal volcanic cone, waterfall, tea fields, beach + offshore island. Strongest nature-fit tour in catalogue.
